text:
<think>I should begin by addressing the common misconception that Greek and Roman statues were always plain white marble.</think>
Many people today assume that Greek and Roman statues were originally pure white, but this is not accurate.
<think>Now I should clarify what the true appearance of these statues was during their creation.</think>
In reality, most Greek and Roman statues were painted in vibrant colors.
<think>I should mention the term "polychromy" and explain briefly.</think>
This use of color is called "polychromy," meaning "many colors."
<think>It would be helpful to explain what kinds of colors and details were typically added to the statues.</think>
Artists would paint skin tones, hair, eyes, clothing, and decorative patterns directly onto the marble or bronze.
<think>I should mention why these colors are not visible on most statues today.</think>
Over centuries, exposure to weather, cleaning, and time caused most of the paint to wear away, leaving behind the bare surfaces we see in museums now.
<think>I want to reinforce the idea that colorful statues were the norm in ancient times, not the exception.</think>
Therefore, at the time of their creation, Greek and Roman statues were often vividly colored and much more lifelike than the white statues we are familiar with today.
